metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving step for AC2: e.g., 'Temporarily mutate one leaf to send the wrong target/drop a field; run `make test` and confirm the new behavioral test for that leaf fails RED; then revert.' Document this as part of the review checklist or as an explicit verification step in the plan.
findings.finding: AC2 ('A leaf that targets the wrong entity / drops a field / returns the wrong follow-on fails its test') asserts a property of the test suite but provides no proving command that demonstrates the tests actually catch those defects. `make test` green is necessary but not sufficient — it passes even if the new tests are written so loosely they would not detect the named failure modes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'A leaf that targets the wrong entity / drops a field / returns the wrong follow-on fails its test.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'AC2-verify: To confirm AC2, temporarily modify one leaf (e.g., change its target entity) and run `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/orchestrate/test_leaves_registry_coverage.py`; the leaf's behavioral test must fail. Revert after verification.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command (or make it an explicit implementation requirement) that the parametrize fixture derives its leaf list directly from `_LEAVES` at import time, so `pytest --collect-only | grep leaf_name` would reveal any uncovered leaf — or add an explicit assertion that `len(tested_leaves) == len(_LEAVES)`.
findings.finding: AC4 (`make test green`) is the sole proving command, but it does not verify that EVERY leaf in `_LEAVES` has a corresponding behavioral test — a parametrized suite could skip newly added leaves silently if the parametrize list is manually maintained rather than derived from `_LEAVES` at runtime.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: '`make test` green.' / AC1: 'Each registry leaf has a behavioral test asserting its expected external effect.'
findings.suggested_fix: Require in the What/Implementation section: 'The parametrize decorator MUST derive its argument list from `_LEAVES` directly (e.g., `@pytest.mark.parametrize("leaf", _LEAVES.values())`) so coverage is automatically complete as the registry grows.' Add a prove step: `pytest --collect-only -q tests/.../test_leaves_registry_coverage.py | grep -c "leaf"` and assert the count equals `len(_LEAVES)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which of the 12 `_LEAVES` entries already have direct leaf-level behavioral tests (mock Jira client asserting exact effects) and call out only the true coverage gaps (at minimum `_apply_outbound_probe`, `_apply_outbound_conflict`, and `_apply_outbound_delete`) so the implementation targets real holes rather than duplicating existing coverage.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the several pre-existing leaf-level behavioral tests already in the repo (e.g. `test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py`, `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py`, `test_applier_clean_label.py`, `test_applier_repair_property.py`, `test_conflict_filing_deferred.py`, `test_inbound_apply_bound_target.py`, `test_hard_delete_recreate.py`) that already cover a majority of the 12 `_LEAVES` entries with mock-client assertions. Without acknowledging them, implementors cannot tell which leaves actually lack behavioral coverage (the real gaps appear to be `_apply_outbound_probe`, `_apply_outbound_conflict`, and `_apply_outbound_delete` at the leaf level) and risk either duplicating existing tests or leaving the true gaps unaddressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Existing coverage inventory' subsection to the plan listing which leaves are already covered by leaf-level behavioral tests and marking the 2–3 true gaps (outbound probe, outbound conflict, outbound delete) as the primary new-test targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge existing per-leaf behavioral coverage (test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py, test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py, test_outbound_update_combined.py) in the plan's Scope section and narrow the new work to the leaves with no current behavioral test (at minimum: outbound_delete, outbound_probe, outbound_conflict).
findings.finding: The plan calls for behavioral tests on 'every leaf in _LEAVES' without acknowledging that dense per-leaf behavioral coverage already exists for all inbound leaves (test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py) and outbound_create / outbound_update (test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py, test_outbound_update_combined.py). Without this prior-art acknowledgment, the implementer may duplicate existing tests rather than targeting the real residual gap (outbound_delete, outbound_probe, outbound_conflict have no dedicated behavioral leaf test), and reviewers cannot tell whether ACs are met by the existing tests or require new ones.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prior art' or 'Gap analysis' subsection under ## Scope listing which leaves already have behavioral tests and which do not, e.g.: 'Already covered: inbound_{create,update,delete,repair_property,conflict,clean_label}, outbound_{create-rollback,update}. Gap: outbound_{delete,probe,conflict}. New tests must cover at least these three; extending existing inbound tests is optional if the AST heuristic is retired.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether removing the AST heuristic test is REQUIRED or optional; if optional, define the minimum condition (e.g., 'may be retained ONLY after all leaves have a behavioral test') so the decision criterion is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The title promises 'replace' the heuristic, but the plan body hedges with 'may keep the AST heuristic as an explicitly-supplemental guard' and AC #3 conditions its treatment on whether it is retained. An implementer is left uncertain whether deletion of the old test is required or optional, which could result in the old heuristic remaining as primary coverage if the implementer takes the permissive path.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'may keep the AST heuristic as an explicitly-supplemental guard' to 'the AST heuristic MUST be demoted (relabeled or removed) once every leaf has a passing behavioral test; it must not remain as the primary coverage signal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default decision on the AST heuristic in the Scope section (e.g., 'KEEP and relabel' or 'DROP entirely') so the executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The Scope section uses 'may keep the AST heuristic as an explicitly-supplemental guard,' deferring the keep-or-drop decision to the executor rather than stating a default. This is a real design choice with downstream consequences (whether to write AC3's relabeling work at all).
findings.location: Scope section, sentence: 'may keep the AST heuristic as an explicitly-supplemental guard, not behavioral coverage.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'may keep the AST heuristic as an explicitly-supplemental guard' with a definitive statement, e.g.: 'Retain the AST heuristic and relabel its test as a supplemental structural guard (not behavioral coverage).' Then make AC3 unconditional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a follow-on kind vocabulary table to the plan (suppress_pair, create_after_hard_delete, schema_drift_signal) and map each leaf to its expected follow-on shape so the per-leaf effect matrix cells are fully defined.
findings.finding: The plan mandates asserting 'follow-ons' for each leaf but never enumerates the follow-on `kind` vocabulary (suppress_pair, create_after_hard_delete, schema_drift_signal) or specifies which leaves emit which kind — leaving the effect matrix cells undefined for this dimension.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria AC1 and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What or ## Acceptance Criteria section, add a table: leaf → expected follow_on.kind (or None). E.g. _apply_inbound_conflict → {kind: suppress_pair, local_id, jira_key}; _apply_inbound_delete (hard_delete branch) → {action: create_after_hard_delete, target, local_id}; inbound_repair_property (failure) → {kind: schema_drift_signal, issue_key, reason}; all others → None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the required failure-mode test cases per leaf in the plan (at minimum: client=None stub path, JiraAPIError-404 soft-path for leaves that handle it, and DirectionMismatchError for each leaf's direction guard).
findings.finding: The plan says each leaf test must assert 'failure behavior' but never enumerates the failure modes that must be covered per leaf — leaving the processing protocol for the failure dimension of the matrix undefined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit the existing per-leaf behavioral tests (test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py, test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py, test_outbound_update_combined.py, test_applier_clean_label.py, test_applier_repair_property.py) and scope the new 'effect matrix' work to only the leaves that genuinely lack behavioral coverage (e.g. outbound_probe, outbound_conflict) rather than rebuilding coverage that already exists.
findings.finding: The plan proposes building a 'per-leaf effect matrix' from scratch without acknowledging the substantial per-leaf behavioral test coverage already present in the codebase (test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py for all 7 inbound leaves; test_apply_outbound_create_rollback.py, test_outbound_update_combined.py, test_applier_clean_label.py, test_applier_repair_property.py for outbound leaves), risking duplicate tests that rebuild rather than extend existing infrastructure.
findings.location: ## What / ## Scope / AC line 'Each registry leaf has a behavioral test asserting its expected external effect.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Gaps to fill' subsection under ## Scope that enumerates which leaves already have behavioral tests and which do not (per the grep above), so the implementation targets only the delta. Existing tests satisfying AC1/AC2 should be cited as already meeting those criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the **What** or **Why** section explaining why the mock-Jira-client behavioral matrix at the AcliClient/leaf boundary is the right approach (e.g., lowest blast-radius, matches existing test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py pattern, isolates leaf logic from network calls).
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section explains the deficiency being replaced but provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen approach — per-leaf behavioral test with mock Jira client at the AcliClient boundary — is the right mechanism. An implementer cannot tell from the plan why this boundary was chosen over, say, an integration-level HTTP stub or a higher-level reconciler test.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the **What** section: 'This boundary (mocking at the AcliClient interface) is chosen because it isolates each leaf's dispatch logic from Jira network calls, mirrors the pattern already established in test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py for inbound leaves, and keeps test blast-radius confined to the leaf under test.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
